utterances.text: you know we can refer you to pain medicine if it really is getting that complicated but for this current period we're gon na put you on some robaxin it's gon na be fifteen hundred milligrams and you're gon na take that six to eight hours every six to eight hours and that really should help kinda relax the muscles in the area take off some of that tension and really help with that pain if you're noticing that the robaxin still is n't helping maybe then we can start we can like start using like a heat pad or maybe some icyhot the biofreeze is a really good one to kinda help with that and then we will refer you to physical therapy i think with the mri we can start evaluating maybe some additional steps so rather than you having to like take that fentanyl because i do n't want you to be in danger right like people i do n't want you to get rubbed so what we could explore are local injections right and we can refer you to pain medication and see about like locally injecting the area and that should be able to help you out hopefully with this

detail_list.summary: Neck pain.
The doctor asks how many car crashes the patient has had recently.
The patient reports being fine after the second crash, having surgery after the third, and being fine until the most recent crash.
The patient reports the most recent accident occurred in February while returning from a pain clinic for neck and back pain.
The doctor asks the patient to rate their pain on a scale of one to ten.
The patient rates their pain as 12 out of 10.
The doctor asks if the patient has been prescribed any medication for their pain.
The patient reports they were recently prescribed fentanyl.
The patient has not received a fentanyl prescription for several weeks.
The doctor asks if the patient is experiencing stiffness.
The doctor asks the patient to describe the quality of their pain.
The patient describes the pain as sharp and incapacitating, noting they are unable to work.
The doctor asks if the patient is having headaches.
The doctor asks if the patient is experiencing dizziness.
The doctor asks if the patient has visual disturbances.
The doctor asks if the patient has any numbness.
The patient reports numbness in their left arm and right leg.
The doctor asks if the patient is experiencing spasms.
The doctor begins the physical exam of the cervical spine.
The doctor asks if there is pain on palpation of the bony process.
The doctor asks if there is pain on palpation of the muscle.
The doctor notes pain on palpation of the bony process and muscle, and observes moderate range of motion.
The doctor notes no bruising, swelling, or lacerations on the neck.
The doctor asks if the patient is experiencing fatigue.
The doctor reports that neck x-rays reveal no fractures.
The doctor assesses the patient with a neck sprain.
The doctor orders an MRI for further evaluation.
The doctor instructs the patient to use ice, heat, IcyHot, or Biofreeze, and provides a home exercise program.
The doctor prescribes Robaxin 1500 mg every 6 to 8 hours.
The doctor advises the patient they are off work until MRI results are reviewed.

case_description: neck pain; body spasms;arm and leg numbness
